verses.text: At Shushan, Nehemiah hears from Hanani that the returned remnant is in distress and Jerusalem's wall lies broken and its gates burned. The bad news pierces a heart far from home. Those who love God's people feel their afflictions as their own, though comfort surrounds them.

At Shushan, Nehemiah hears from Hanani that the returned remnant is in distress and Jerusalem's wall lies broken and its gates burned. The bad news pierces a heart far from home. Those who love God's people feel their afflictions as their own, though comfort surrounds them.

At Shushan, Nehemiah hears from Hanani that the returned remnant is in distress and Jerusalem's wall lies broken and its gates burned. The bad news pierces a heart far from home. Those who love God's people feel their afflictions as their own, though comfort surrounds them.
Hearing it, Nehemiah sits down and weeps, mourning, fasting, and praying for days. Grief drives him not to despair but to God. The first response of a godly heart to ruin is not a plan but a prayer.

He prays to the great and awesome God who keeps covenant, confessing the sins of Israel and of his own house. He counts himself among the guilty. True intercession owns the people's sin as its own before pleading for mercy.

He prays to the great and awesome God who keeps covenant, confessing the sins of Israel and of his own house. He counts himself among the guilty. True intercession owns the people's sin as its own before pleading for mercy.

He prays to the great and awesome God who keeps covenant, confessing the sins of Israel and of his own house. He counts himself among the guilty. True intercession owns the people's sin as its own before pleading for mercy.

Nehemiah pleads God's own promise: if they return to Him, He will gather them from the farthest place to His chosen dwelling. He holds God to His word. The surest ground in prayer is the promise God has already spoken.

Nehemiah pleads God's own promise: if they return to Him, He will gather them from the farthest place to His chosen dwelling. He holds God to His word. The surest ground in prayer is the promise God has already spoken.

He asks God to prosper His servant and grant him mercy before 'this man' — the king he serves as cupbearer. He prays before he acts. The believer brings even his daily duty and his dealings with the great to the throne of God first.
